content.text:
A review of human reliability assessment methods for proposed application
in quantitative risk analysis of offshore industries

content.text: Nilesh Ade, S. Camille Peres *
Mary Kay O’Connor Process Safety Center, Artie McFerrin Department of Chemical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, 77843-3122, USA
A R T I C L E I N F O
A B S T R A C T
1. Introduction

The offshore oil and gas industry is regarded as a high-risk domain
due to the associated hazards of exploration, drilling, storage and pro-
cessing of hydrocarbons (Broni-Bediako and Amorin, 2010). This risk is
reflected in the high likelihood and consequences associated with
offshore incidents as observed in the period 1964–2015, wherein 2440
oil spills in the Gulf of Mexico amounted to over 12 million gallons of the
oil spill (Howell, 2018). These statistics exclude the Deepwater Horizon
incident that itself resulted in 130 million gallons of oil spill along-with
11 fatalities (The Ocean Portal Team, 2018). This incident was regarded
as the largest oil spill in US history (Graham et al., 2011). Interestingly,
even minor offshore incidents can lead to significant economic losses as
such incidents can contribute to an increase in the downtime of the fa-
cility leading to a loss in production.

To mitigate the likelihood of such loss of life and economic resources,
it is important to analyze risk methodically. The risk is defined as the
product of the likelihood of an undesired scenario and the consequence
associated with it (CCPS, 2000). The quantification of risk is tradition-
ally achieved by quantitative risk analysis (QRA). Based on the assess-
ment of the quantified risk, management of risk is achieved by improved
design and operation of the facility (CCPS, 2000). However, quantitative
risk analysis of offshore facilities is inherently a complex problem due to
the socio-technical nature of these systems and to their dynamics. The
design and operation of offshore facilities involves interactions between
technical and human factors (Skogdalen and Vinnem, 2011). In complex
industrial systems, it has been observed that around 80% of the incidents
occur wholly or partly due to the way humans interact with the sys-
tem—often referred to has human error (Baron, 1988). Human-system
interactions have been identified as key contributors to incidents in
the offshore industry as well (Gordon, 1998). One of the highlights from
the incident investigation report of the Deepwater Horizon incident was
the need to systematically identify and manage human’s interactions
(both with technology and each other) within these sociotechnical sys-
tems (Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board, 2016):

“The committee recommends that MMS (Minerals Management
Service) place its primary emphasis on detection of potential accident-
producing situations, particularly those involving human factors, oper-
ational procedures, and modifications of equipment and facilities”.

Hence, an effective QRA for the offshore facility should account for
all attributes of these socio-technical systems, i.e., the human, technical
factors, and operational issues. Such integration may allow for accurate
estimations of the likelihood elements of risk and therefore, aid in better
decision-making for improving system safety. However, to achieve this
required integration, it is essential to understand how humans interact
with and within systems from the perspective of system safety. Causes of
reduced human performance (i.e., human error) have been identified in
multiple previous studies. Some of the well-known causes include
inadequate risk perception (Patel et al., 2012), improper decision
making (Manchi et al., 2013), and lack of situation awareness (Di et al.,
2013)—all of these leading to negative impact on the safety perfor-
mance of the system. Reason (1990) identified several classifications of
human errors: slips (actions not carried as intended), lapses (actions
missed), mistakes (inadequate plans leading to undesired outcomes
irrespective of the actions) (Reason, 1990). Cumulatively, human error
is defined as the unintended failure of achieving the planned outcomes
in a form of action, checking, retrieval, transmission, diagnostic and
decision errors (Alkhaldi, M, Pathirage, C and Kulatunga, 2017). How-
ever, it is important to understand that these errors can originate from
multiple levels: the individual, group, and organizational. Interestingly,
the level of the human factor is determined by the nature of human
error. This relationship between levels of the human factor and the
nature of human errors (Gordon, 1998) has been explained through the
Rasmussen’s skill, rule, knowledge (SRK) (Rasmussen, 1993) and is
shown in Table 1:

The incorporation of HRA in QRA has been a subject of interest for
offshore domain (Skogdalen and Vinnem, 2011). A study focusing on 15
QRAs applied in Norway and UK offshore revealed that many of these
methods did not sufficiently address HRA (Skogdalen and Vinnem,
2011). It was also observed that these QRA methods varied substantially
thus making the derived results questionable. To address these gaps, the
goal of this study is aimed to evaluate the HRA methods from the
perspective of their application towards offshore QRA. To achieve this
objective, it is essential to understand the origins of HRA methods, their
classifications and their current applicability in offshore domain. A re-
view pertaining around these elements might provide insights into
appropriate selection of HRA methods for QRA applications based on
specific type of offshore tasks (e.g. maintenance). Similarly, the review
will reflect the current state of the HRA based QRA methods, the im-
provements needed in these methods, the origins for variations (i.e.
uncertainties) and directions for future studies.
2. Method

To achieve the mentioned goals, current literature was reviewed and
potential methods for achieving this goal were identified. These
methods were identified from various safety journals and conference
proceedings. The safety journals included those considered in a recent
study that tried to evaluate the relative ranking of 35 safety journals
based on multiple aspects such as expert opinion and citations (Genserik
Reniers and Yannick Anthone, 2012). Conference proceedings from
various well-known safety conferences such as offshore technology
conference (OTC), research partnership to secure energy for America
(RPSEA), Mary Kay O’Connor process safety center international sym-
posium (MKOPSC), offshore oil and gas producers (OOGP), American
Institute for chemical engineers (AIChE), center for offshore safety
(COS), center for operator performance (COP), international association
for drilling contractors (IADC) and society for petroleum geophysicists
(SPG) were reviewed as well to address the proposed research objective.

To identify relevant articles amongst these journal articles and con-
ference proceedings a combination of sets of keywords were used,
similar to that proposed in a previous review study (Goerlandt et al.,
2017). The first set of keywords were related to QRA and included
“QRA”, “risk analysis”, “risk assessment”, “safety”, “safety assessment”.
The second set of keywords were related to human factors and included
“human”, “human factor”, “human error”, “human and organizational
factor”. These two sets of keywords were used along with the term
“offshore” to limit the search in the offshore domain. Lastly, using the
indexing system of Ei-Compendex, more relevant articles were added
based on the citations (references) of identified articles. The resulting
sets of articles were manually screened considering the review scope and
defined research goals. The manual screening was done based on the
title, abstract and full text (if readily available and the information in the
abstract was limited).
2.1. Traditional methods for human factor integration in QRA

In an effective QRA, the contribution of both technical and human
factors towards risk is evaluated. Specifically, human factors contribute
largely to the likelihood element of risk whereas technical factors
contribute to both the consequence and likelihood element of risk
(CCPS, 2000). The first step in QRA is defining the undesired scenario for
which the risk has to be evaluated (Crowl and Louvar, 2011). This is
followed by identifying the hazards in the process, defining likely sce-
narios, initiating and intermediate events in those and possible adverse
outcomes. Human errors are mainly involved as either initiating or in-
termediate events in such scenarios (CCPS, 2000). The likelihood of
human errors (as initiating or intermediate events) is conventionally
evaluated through human failure databases, human reliability analysis
(HRA) (Steijn et al., 2017) or human error estimation (CCPS, 2000).
Human error estimation is often done using expert judgment or based on
arbitrary rules (Steijn et al., 2017) and therefore, the overall QRA results
deduced based on such estimation are questionable due to the associated
uncertainty. The use of failure databases for estimation of human error
likelihood is deemed superior to the mentioned human error estimation
approach as the human failure data is generally collected through lab-
oratory studies, field studies and expert judgment or a combination of
these methods and is, therefore, equally or more practicable (CCPS,
2000). However, these databases are afflicted with uncertainties due to
variations in the type of tasks, environmental conditions, quality of
controls and displays, motivation, stress, skill, training, and quality of
instructions (Meister, 1978; NRC, 1998). The HRA approach for human
error likelihood method is the widely recommended approach (Bell and
Holroyd, 2009) wherein qualitative and quantitative methods are used
for human error identification, quantification, and reduction. These
methods fully/partially account for the mentioned variations and
therefore, the deduced QRA results from these methods are more plau-
sible (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). Interestingly, some of the HRA methods
are completely/partially based on expert judgment and human failure
databases. An example of a commonly used HRA method is shown in
Fig. 1:

Competence |  | Action errors
Stress |  | Checking errors

content.text:
The HRA methods are generally classified as first-generation, second-
generation and third-generation methods (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). In
first-generation methods, humans are treated equivalently to mechani-
cal components in the sense that humans are assumed to have natural
flaws that result in the logical failure of tasks (Di et al., 2013). The
first-generation methods are characterized by indirect treatment of
context, the absence of a cognitive model, focus on the phenomenology
of human actions, the binary representation of human performance
(success/failure) and emphasis on quantifying the likelihood of incor-
rect performance (Kim, 2001). They are generally based on skill and
rule-based errors and therefore, ignore context and organizational
human factors. Examples of these methods include a technique for
human error rate prediction (THERP) (Swain and Guttmann, 1983),
human cognition reliability (HCR) (Madonna et al., 2009) and accident
sequence evaluation program (ASEP) (Kim and Jung, 2003). The
second-generation methods were developed to counter the deficiencies
of the first-generation method. Specifically, these methods are based on
cognitive modeling. They are focused on the causes of human errors as
opposed to their frequency and emphasize the interactions of factors
that lead to these errors (Iannone et al., 2004). These factors are referred
to as performance shaping factors (PSFs) i.e. factors related to the
environment, human (operator) and task that has the potential to cause
human error. Essentially, PSFs in first generation methods focus only on
the operator characteristics whereas, PSFs in second-generation
methods focus on the task and environment characteristics as well. Ex-
amples of second-generation methods include cognitive reliability and
error analysis method (CREAM) (Hollnagel, 1989) and standardized
plant analysis risk–human reliability analysis method (SPAR-H) (Boring
and Blackman, 2007). However, even these methods suffer from certain
deficiencies: lack of empirical data for model development and valida-
tion, inaccurate cognitive modeling, variability in implementation and
reliance on expert judgment (Griffith and Mahadevan, 2011). Lastly, to
overcome these drawbacks of second-generation methods,
third-generation methods were developed. An example of these methods
includes nuclear action reliability assessment (NARA) (Kirwan et al.,
2004). These methods are based on specific databases and therefore, the
results derived from these methods have relatively lower uncertainties
(Di et al., 2013). Overall, in recent years, there has been an increased
emphasis on PSFs and their contribution towards HRA as seen in the
generational advancement of these methods. However understanding
the interactions and dependencies amongst PSFs has proven to be a
challenge towards HRA (Di et al., 2013). It is also important to note that
ambiguities exist with respect to classification of HRA methods amongst
the three generations (De Oliveira et al., 2017). The general notion of
generations amongst the HRA methods is primarily used by the HRA
community to describe chronology of development as opposed to their
underlying methodologies. However, for the specific goals of this study
described previously, the above described definitions of the 3 genera-
tions will be followed.
2.2. HRA methods implemented in offshore industries

Although HRA methods are extensively used in offshore industries,
they were primarily developed for the nuclear domain. Specifically,
HRA was developed for probabilistic risk assessment of nuclear weapons
assembly in the 1960s (Cross and Youngblood, 2017). Subsequently,
HRA was adapted for risk assessment for nuclear power plants and was
introduced in the nuclear regulatory framework through a
first-generation HRA method; THERP (Fleming, 1974; Swain and Gutt-
mann, 1983). Currently, HRA is used in various safety-critical domains
such as aerospace, rail, transportation and oil, and gas. A study by the
UK health and safety executive identified 17 HRA methods that were of
potential use to major hazard authoritative organizations (Bell and
Holroyd, 2009). These methods included first, second and
third-generation methods. Interestingly, a similar study was carried by
the international association of oil and gas producers (IOGP) to identify
and propose HRA methods that were suitable for oil and gas QRA (IOGP,
2010). Based on this study, a standard referred as IOGP 434-5 was put
forth that recommended the application of 3 specific HRA methods. The
17 identified methods by UK-HSE, their generation, brief description,
applicable domains and their presence/absence in the IOGP 434-5
standard is shown in Table 2.

As observed from Table 2, most HRA methods were developed for the
nuclear domain. Although both nuclear and offshore domain are similar
in the sense that both are high-risk domains with the potential to cause
incidents with severe consequences to personnel and environment, HRA
methods developed for a nuclear domain cannot be directly applied for
offshore applications because of certain inherent differences between
these domains. These differences include highly centralized control
nature of nuclear power plants, the involvement of exploration,
extraction, transportation and refining activities in offshore, the differ-
ence in the principle technology and differences in magnitude and types
of hazards involved (Boring, 2015). In order to counter these differences
and develop an HRA method for oil and gas domain, the Norwegian oil
industry and Norwegian research council undertook the Petro-HRA

content.description: This is a hierarchical flowchart illustrating how performance shaping factors (PSFs) influence human error probability in diagnostic tasks. The chart categorizes tasks into 'Diagnosis' and then further breaks down into 'Experience' and 'Ergonomics', each with subcategories of 'High', 'Low', 'Misleading', 'Poor', and 'Good'. It quantifies the impact of these factors on human error probability, showing that high experience reduces the error rate to 0.5%, while poor ergonomics increases it to 10%.
content.caption: Fig. 1. A commonly used HRA method (SPAR-H) (Gertman et al., 2005).

content.text:
Acronym | HRA method | Generation | Domain | Presence in IOGP 434-5 | Description
--- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---
THERP | Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction (Castiglia et al., 2015; Kirwan, 1996; Zimolong,1992) | First | Nuclear/ generic | Yes | THERP assesses human reliability through task analysis followed by identification, representation,and quantification of human error probability.
ASEP | Accident Sequence Evaluation Program (Kirwan,1994; Mock and Scherrer, 2004) | First | Nuclear | No | ASEP involves pre-accident screening HRA,post-accident screening HRA,and nominal HRA.ASEP requires relatively less training and time than THERP.
HEART | Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique ( Kirwan, 1988; Lyons et al., 2004; Williams,1988) | First | Generic | Yes | HEART involves classification of each task within 9 generic task types that have an associated nominal human error potential.The effect of 40 different error-producing conditions (PSFs) is considered on these nominal human
APJ | Absolute Probability Judgement (Embrey and Kirwan, 1983; Kirwan and Taylor-Adams, 1995) | First | Generic | Yes | error potentials. APJ involves estimation of human reliability through single expert or group expert methods.Group APJ involve aggregated individual, Delphi, nominal group and
PC | Paired Comparisons (Embrey and Kirwan,1983; Park and Lee, 2008; Zhou and Kou, 2010) | First | Generic | No | consensus group methods. PC method differs from the APJ method in the sense that experts make simple comparative judgments rather than absolute judgments.Each expert compares possble pairs of
SLIM- | Success likelihood index methodology,multi-attribute utility decomposition (Boring and Gertman, 2005; |  | Nuclear/ |  | error and determines the error that is more probable. In SLIM,experts determine success likelihood index representing the overall belief of that expert regarding the
MAUD | Calixto et al., 2013; Tu et al., 2015) |  | generic |  | positive/negative effect of PSF on the task under consideration.These indices are transformed into HEP using logarithmic relationship.MAUD is a computer-based procedure for SLIM.
HRMS | Human Reliability Management System (Kirwan,1994; Swain, 1990) | First | Nuclear | No | HRMS is a fully computerized HRA system that supports error identification and reduction.It essentially involves task analysis,error analysis,and PSF quantification.HRMS
JHEDI | Justified Human Error Data Information (Aalipour et al., 2016; Kirwan, 1994) | First | Nuclear | No | utilizes multipliers for PSFs and extrapolation for HRA. JHEDI is a simplified form of HRMS wherein lesser PSF multipliers are used. It is conceptually like THERP and HEART.
 | INTENT (Gertman et al., 1992) | First | Nuclear | No | INTENT classifies error amongst 20 types each with upper and lower estimates of error probabilities.The effect of 11 PSF on these nominal probabilities for HRA is evaluated.
ATHEANA | A Technique for Human Error Analysis (Boring and Gertman, 2005; Thompson et al., 1997) | Second | Nuclear/ generic | No | ATHEANA involves the search schemes for“error-forcing contexts"based on probabilistic risk assessment, human factors and psychology,and accident analysis.Human error is evaluated based on the identified contexts.
CREAM | Cognitive Reliability and Error Analysis Method ( Collier and Andresen, 2001; He et al., 2008; Hollnagel, 1989; Konstandinidou et al., 2006) | Second | Nuclear/ generic | No | In CREAM, common performance condition level is determined to evaluate performance reliability.This is followed by the building of cognitive demands profile.
CAHR | Connectionism Assessment of Human Reliability ( Hallbert et al., 2004; Le Bot, 2004) | Second | Generic | No | CAHR is a database system for analyzing operational disturbances.It combines event analysis and assessment from experience for HRA.
 | Commisson Errors Search and Assessment (Podofillini et al., 2013; Reer et al., 2004) |  | Nuclear | No | CESA involves cataloging key action responses to plant actions followed by a systematic review of context-action combinations to obtain a set of situations with errors of commission opportunities.These situations are later analyzed in detail.
CODA | Conclusions from occurrences by descriptions of actions (Reer,1997) | Second | Nuclear | No | CODA method uses an open list of guidelines to compile a short story that includes all unusual occurrences and their essential context without excessive technical details.
MERMOS | Method d'Evaluation de la Realisation des Missions Operateur pour la Surete (Le Bot, 2O04; Meyer et al., 2007) | Second | Nuclear | No | MERMOS method only utilizes emergency operation during 4 h after incident initiator.It evaluates the adequacy of human performance of emergency operations system that comprises of operating crew,operating procedures,and
SPAR-H | (Simplified Plant Analysis Risk Human Reliability Assessment (Boring and Blackman, 2007; Groth and Swiler, 2013; Liu and Li, 2014; Van De Merwe et al., | Second | Nuclear/ generic | No | man-machine interfaces. In SPAR-Hmethod, human activity is categorized into action and diagnosis tasks,each with associated base error rate. The effect of 8 PSFs is evaluated on these base error rates.
NARA | 2012) Nuclear Action Reliability Assessment (Kirwan et al., 2004) | Third | Nuclear | No | NARA is primarily based on HEART methodology.It consists of 14 generic task types and 18 error producing contexts.

content.text:
project (Bye et al., 2016). A detailed discussion about Petro-HRA has
been provided in a later section. PSFs typically used in nuclear and
offshore industry (Bye et al., 2016) are shown in Fig. 2.
2.3. First-generation HRA methods in offshore industries

The review involved in this study revealed that first generation
methods were extensively used in the offshore industry. These methods
included SLIM, human error probability index (HEPI, a SLIM-based
model), HEART and THERP. Specifically, SLIM and SLIM-based HEPI
were implemented for emergency scenarios as the SLIM method is
inherently based on a ‘what-if’ approach which makes it applicable for
such scenarios (Khan et al., 2006). SLIM utilizes expert judgment to
determine human error probabilities (HEP). The SLIM model was suc-
cessfully implemented to determine HEP for offshore platform musters
(DiMattia et al., 2005). Specifically, the model considered 3 muster
scenarios: man overboard, gas release and fire and explosion. 24 experts
provided their judgments in the form of weights and ratings for six PSFs:
stress, complexity, training, experience, event factors and environment
factors. This data was then converted into HEP using SLIM protocol
(DiMattia, 2004). Another SLIM-based model, HEPI was subsequently
developed and validated for the Ocean Odyssey case study (Khan et al.,
2006). HEPI utilized the same six PSFs from the offshore muster study.
However, the HEPI model differed from the core SLIM model in the
sense that HEPI utilized SLI reference graphs to determine HEP. Also, the
model utilized a risk matrix comprising of consequence severity and HEP
to evaluate the overall risk. The application of reference graphs in HEPI
facilitated standardization of the SLIM model and the implementation of
risk matrix provided insight in the relation between HEP and human
factors such as training, design, safety systems, and procedures. Another
emergency scenario; offshore evacuation was analyzed using the HEART
method (Deacon et al., 2013). However, in this study along-with expert
judgment, learnings from incident investigations are also used for HEP
calculation. However, limited application of the first-generation method
for modeling non-emergency scenarios was observed in this review. This
included an integrated approach consisting of SLIM and THERP for HRA
during maintenance activity in an offshore platform (Abbassi et al.,
2015). It is important to note that offshore maintenance is a transient
operation as well and therefore the ‘what-if’ applicability is also suitable
for such operation.

content.description: This is a quadrant diagram illustrating the interdependencies of operator characteristics, administrative and organizational characteristics, process characteristics, and task characteristics in a technical system. Each quadrant lists specific factors that influence these categories, such as competence, fatigue, work environment, and procedures, work permit, and work practice for the respective quadrants. The diagram visually represents how these factors collectively impact the overall performance and safety of the system.
content.caption: Fig. 2. PSFs typically used in nuclear and offshore industries (Boring, 2010; Sklet et al., 2006a).

content.text:
In a general context, first-generation HRA methods have been
observed to only focus on external behavior and errors while ignoring
the cognitive reasons and mechanisms behind these errors (Str¨ater et al.,
2004). Specifically, these methods give less importance to the environ-
ment, organizational factors and other relevant factors (Hollnagel, 1989;
Mosleh and Chang, 2004; Strater ¨ et al., 2004). Moreover, the absence of
a standardized method for accounting expert judgments makes the re-
sults deduced from these methods questionable. These deficiencies of
first-generation methods lead to higher estimates of HEP with wide
uncertainty bounds (Hollnagel, 1989).
2.4. Second-generation HRA methods in offshore industries

Although SLIM and HEPI proved to be applicable for the offshore
domain, they were mainly designed/applied for emergency scenarios. A
study was conducted to reevaluate HRA methods and identify methods
that were appropriate for normal design and operation (Gould et al.,
2012). This study served as a cornerstone for evaluation of HRA methods
from the perspective of the offshore domain. As a part of this study,
HEART, CREAM, and SPAR-H models were tested for the offshore
domain. It was observed that both HEART and CREAM suffered from
low interrater reliability, unreliable results (i.e. unreliable error proba-
bilities) and were too complicated to apply. It was concluded from this
study that SPAR-H method was the most applicable method for the
offshore domain as it is non-resource intensive, provides specific guid-
ance on the assignment of weights to PSFs and provides standard
worksheets that promoted consistency. SPAR-H being a
second-generation HRA method is based on cognitive modeling. It is
specifically based on explicit information processing model of human
performance wherein the perception processing response is assumed to
depend on information inflow, task demand characteristics, environ-
mental and situational factors, external, short-term and long-term
memory (Blackman et al., 2008; Boring and Blackman, 2007). Howev-
er, due to its nuclear origin, SPAR-H was still found to be not fully
applicable to oil and gas domain. The method requires differentiating
diagnosis and action tasks which is quite complex in the offshore
domain. Also, the PSF for ‘available time’ in SPAR-H is calibrated for
nuclear control room tasks and might be inaccurate for offshore tasks
such as drilling that deal with higher time-scales. These deficiencies in
SPAR-H served as motivation for the development for Petro-HRA, a
SPAR-H based HRA method optimized for petroleum domain (Bye et al.,
2016).

Petro-HRA method broadly consists of scenario definition,
qualitative data collection, task analysis, human error identification,
modeling, quantification, and reduction (Bye et al., 2016). However, a
distinguishing feature of Petro-HRA is its non-linear nature as shown in
Fig. 3. Petro-HRA involves iterations within and between steps
throughout the method to ensure robust analysis. This method is specific
for the petroleum industry in the sense that it provides context-specific
guidance on qualitative data collection and analysis, quantitative anal-
ysis, and HRA integration in QRA. However, Petro-HRA has its own
limitations. The Petro-HRA method has been designed for post-initiator
scenarios but incidents can occur from pre-initiator events such as
maintenance errors. In a simpler scene, human error may be contrib-
uting cause for an incident (pre-initiator) as opposed to a response to an
incident (post-initiator). The Petro-HRA method has not been tested and
validated for such pre-incident scenarios. More importantly, the method
is designed for control room tasks and therefore, the PSFs for this
method must be adjusted appropriately for non-control room tasks.

In comparison to first-generation methods, second-generation
methods such as SPAR-H/Petro-HRA try to explain explicit behavior
through cognitive modeling. These methods explain the in-
terdependencies amongst the PSFs and interaction amongst these factors
that lead to human errors (Iannone et al., 2004). However, many of the
second-generation methods lack theoretical or experimental basis (Di
et al., 2013). Specifically, from a quantitative perspective, most of these
methods involve implicit relationships between PSFs and HEP (Mosleh
and Chang, 2004). The second-generation methods have not been
empirically validated as opposed to first-generation methods that have
been largely validated (Kirwan, 1996, 1997; Kirwan et al., 1997). Lastly,
it is important to note that ambiguities exist with respect to the classi-
fication of SPAR-H method. Past studies mention SPAR-H as a
first-generation method (Alvarenga et al., 2014; Bell and Holroyd, 2009;

Garg et al., 2017) due to its reliance on nominal human error proba-
bilities to predict human reliability. However, several other studies (Di
et al., 2013; Wang, 2013; Yao, 2013) classify it as a second-generation
method owing to its reliance on cognitive factors for estimating those
human error probabilities.
2.5. Third-generation HRA methods in offshore industries

Despite being based on cognitive modeling, the second-generation
methods suffer from severe drawbacks. These drawbacks include lack
of empirical data for model development and validation, inadequate
human behavior modeling, large variability in implementation, heavy
reliance on expert judgment in the selection of PSFs and their imple-
mentation (Kim et al., 2006). These drawbacks can be countered by the
development of third-generation methods. These methods rely on
methodological tools based on specific human reliability databases to
reduce uncertainty in HRA (Di et al., 2013). In a simpler sense,
third-generation methods are improved versions of first-generation
methods with industry-specific data that is valid and reproducible.
However, currently only a single third-generation method exists; NARA
(Refer Table 2) which is based on the databases: human event repository
and analysis (HERA) (Halbert and Gertmann, 2004) and the human
factors information system (HFIS). It is important to note that, this
method has only been implemented for the nuclear domain yet and has
not been adapted for the offshore domain. Moreover, NARA is a British
Energy proprietary tool and is not available publicly.
2.6. HRA integration in offshore QRA
This review study revealed various studies that effectively integrated

content.description: This is a flowchart depicting the Petro-HRA (Petrochemical Human Reliability Analysis) method steps for assessing human error in industrial processes. The chart outlines seven sequential stages: scenario definition, qualitative data collection, task analysis, human error identification, human error modeling, human error quantification, and human error reduction. It also illustrates the inputs and outputs of the method, including QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment), recommendations for installation, and the integration of human factor engineering with QRA. The flowchart visually represents the structured approach to identifying, quantifying, and mitigating human errors within petrochemical operations.
content.caption: Fig. 3. Petro-HRA method (Bye et al., 2016).

content.text:
HRA with QRA. However, The HRA methods that were integrated were
found to be mainly first and second-generation methods. These HRA
methods were not the well-known methods described in Table 2. The
QRA methods that were used to incorporate these HRA methods include
safety barrier analysis (SBA) (Sklet et al., 2006a), Bayesian network (BN)
(Musharraf et al., 2013), hybrid causal logic (HCL) (Røed et al., 2009)
and functional resonance analysis method (FRAM) (Toroody et al.,
2016).

One of the earlier methods for HRA-QRA integration for the offshore
domain was the development of barrier and operational risk analysis
model (BORA) (Sklet et al., 2006a, 2006b). The BORA model utilizes a
second-generation HRA approach with SBA to perform a qualitative and
quantitative risk analysis of the platform-specific hydrocarbon release
frequency. The BORA model broadly consists of release scenario
modeling, safety barrier performance modeling, risk quantification,
identifying, weighing and scoring of PSFs and adjustment of risk for the
specific platform under consideration. The PSFs used in this model in-
volves the characteristics of the human (operator), task and environ-
ment thus, making the method second-generation. Overall the model
consists of 27 PSFs, However PSFs used for a specific risk study are
selected on a case-by-case basis and their weighing is carried through
expert judgment. BORA is advantageous in the sense that it helps in
understanding the effect of technical, human, operational and organi-
zational PSFs on various safety barriers and overall risk of the system.
Thus, BORA can also be used to study the effect of risk-reducing mea-
sures on release frequency. One of the major disadvantages of this model
is its barrier-centric nature of HRA that may overlook pre-initiator
events (Boring, 2015) similar to Petro-HRA.

Another major disadvantage of the BORA model is that it assumes
that PSFs have independent effects on the basic events of a scenario.
Thus, this model neglects the interaction effects amongst PSFs towards
basic events for a scenario (Sklet et al., 2006b). These interaction effects
can be effectively modeled using BN. The BN approach can provide a
relatively simple and updatable model that can accommodate uncer-
tainty in the relationships between PSFs. Dynamic BN has been suc-
cessfully applied for QRA in offshore blowouts (Cai et al., 2013). In this
second-generation Dynamic BN approach, human error is described
using human factor barrier failure comprising of individual factor bar-
rier failure, organizational factor barrier failure, and group factor bar-
rier failure. This approach was successful in studying the effect of 37
PSFs and the interactions amongst them. It is interesting to note that the
basic event and relation probabilities of the developed BN are based on
expert judgment. In a similar study, BN was combined with Swiss cheese
model for QRA of offshore platforms (Ren et al., 2008). This model
comprises of 5 levels that describe the causal chain of safety assessment:
root cause level, trigger level, incident level, accident level, and conse-
quence level. Expert judgment is used in this study as well to determine
the probability of nodes in BN, However, the application of Swiss cheese
model reduces the complexity of the model and subsequently, the
amount of data required through expert judgment. In a similar effort to
reduce the dependence on subjective expert judgment, evidence theory
was combined with BN for QRA of an offshore emergency evacuation
(Musharraf et al., 2013). Evidence theory allows the combination of
judgments from multiple experts thus, reducing the associated uncer-
tainty. Interestingly, the results from this study were compared with
SLIM; a method that relies heavily on expert judgment and the results
were found to be similar.

BN are primarily used to study the interrelationships between
different causes that can lead to an undesired scenario through calcu-
lation of posterior probabilities and therefore require the causal struc-
ture leading to a scenario (Alizadeh et al., 2014). Fault trees are applied
for generating the causal structure itself. HCL are models comprising of
BN based on fault trees and are, therefore, able to determine the
contribution of various factors towards final failure (Wang et al., 2011).
HCL model has been successfully applied for HRA integration in QRA of
offshore platforms (Røed et al., 2009). This second-generation approach
is itself based on the BORA model with the main difference being the
application of HCL as opposed to SBA. More importantly, the HCL model
has simpler scoring of PSFs as compared to the BORA model while being
more flexible by allowing causal relationships through several PSF
layers.

SBA, BN, and HCL were therefore successful in integrating second-
generation HRA methods for QRA in the offshore domain. With
respect to first generation methods, it was observed that FRAM could be
utilized for the application of an HRA integration with offshore QRA.
The FRAM approach is suitable for complex socio-technical systems as it
describes such systems in the form of system functions. Each function is
described by six parameters: input, output, time, control, precondition
and resource (Hollnagel, 2004). The mentioned study was implemented
for the application of QRA of the lifting of light structures in the sea with
a crane. SLIM being a first-generation approach, this study mainly
focused on PSFs relevant to skill and knowledge (individual human
factors) and relied on expert-judgment for HEP calculation. The inte-
gration of a first-generation method (such as SLIM) within FRAM is
effective as FRAM can develop a high risk scenario by considering the
non-linear socio-technical interactions in a system and the human error
probabilities associated with the high risk scenario can be accurately
evaluated using the database reliant binary/logical workflow of
first-generation methods.

A key finding from this review is the research mapping of HRA and
HRA-based QRA that can help in the selection of appropriate HRA and
QRA methods. Based on the findings of this review study, a selection
path has been put-forth and shown in Fig. 4.

2.7. Limitations of current HRA-QRA models and recommendations for
future research

This review study provided key insights into the current deficiencies
of HRA models applied in the offshore domain. The first-generation
methods inherently rely on skill and rule-based human errors (Bell
and Holroyd, 2009). These methods assign nominal HEPs based on the
characteristics of the tasks through the effect of PSFs. However, due to
this approach, the effect of task characteristics is more profound than
that of context. In the offshore domain, negligence of context can lead to
inaccurate HRA/QRA results. An example of the importance of context
in offshore HRA is the study that focused on the effect of a cold envi-
ronment on HRA (Noroozi et al., 2014). One context in the offshore
domain can be the physical environment of the facility. The mentioned
study used a revised HEART technique to study the effects of cold
(Arctic) environment on the HRA of post-maintenance tasks of offshore
oil and gas pumps. The study revealed that the value of risk associated
with the task is twice as high as that when the task is done in normal
conditions. Another drawback of first-generation methods is that they
do not consider the effect of characteristics of the task through PSF and
account for their effect only through nominal HEPs (Bell and Holroyd,
2009). This drawback was highlighted in a study focusing on the HRA of
offshore drilling activities wherein PSFs relevant to task was utilized in a
second-generation HRA and these PSFs were observed to have a signif-
icant impact on the consistency of HRA results (Strand and Haskins,
2018).

The key difference between first-generation HRA methods and
second-generation HRA methods is that the prior is behavior based
models whereas the later are the conceptual type (Chang and Mosleh,
2007). The second-generation methods are based on the principle that
human failures (or errors) are predominantly of cognitive nature
(Hollnagel, 1989). It was observed in this review study that, the
implementation of second-generation HRA methods for QRA was on a
much smaller scale as compared to the first-generation methods. One of
the possible reasons for this observation can be that the first-generation
HRA methods are simpler to implement and require less empirical data
due to more reliance on expert judgment. Another contributing reason
can be that first-generation methods have been largely validated
through experiments which are not the case for second-generation
methods (Kirwan, 1996, 1997; Kirwan et al., 1997). Since
first-generation methods provide less importance to task and environ-
mental factors, they are associated with higher HEP estimates with
wider uncertainties (Hollnagel, 1989) and therefore, it is important to
improve current second-generation methods from the perspective of
empirical validation. As second-generation methods essentially deal
with cognitive modeling for human-system integration, their empirical
validation can be achieved with human experimentation (Blanchard and
Fabrycky, 2006). An ideal future direction for research can be the design
of human experiments for cognitive models implemented in these
methods. Specifically, such methods should deal with the effects of
different environmental and task PSFs on various offshore activities.
Another approach can be the development of second-generation
methods that require reduced quantity of data. An example of such
effort is the BN model developed through node reduction algorithms for
the application of HRA (Zwirglmaier et al., 2017). The developed model
can capture the causal paths in cognitive psychology and therefore
provides traceability in HRA.

content.description: This is a flowchart illustrating the selection process for Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) and Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) methods based on the type of offshore activity. It categorizes activities into emergency/transient operations and normal design and operation, guiding through first-generation and second-generation methods, ultimately leading to specific HRA and QRA techniques such as SLIM/THERP, FRAM, SPAR-H, SBA, BN, and HCL.
content.caption: Fig. 4. Selection of appropriate HRA and QRA methods for offshore.

content.text:
An interesting observation of this review study was identifying that
currently, no third-generation methods exist for the offshore domain.
Though, it is important to note that only a single third-generation
method exists for the nuclear domain as well (Table 2). In a simpler
sense, third-generation methods are models that are based on specific
human reliability databases and therefore have reduced uncertainties
associated with them. However, establishing human reliability data-
bases for an offshore domain that relate multiple PSFs relevant to the
offshore domain (Fig. 2) is difficult. Another challenge is that relevant
PSFs vary substantially with respect to tasks and environment. A control
room task in a normal environment will have a different set of relevant
PSFs as compared to an external task in a harsh environment. A future
direction in this sense would be to identify task and environment specific
PSFs for the offshore domain for various tasks and environment, collect
credible data for these identified PSFs and develop cognitive models
based on the collected data. However, such data collection is a monu-
mental task due to various factors such as limitation of resources to
perform experiments, the complexity of experiments, and difficulty in
setting up high-fidelity experiments. A more practical approach can be
the data collection of leading indicators (Tamim et al., 2017). Specif-
ically, leading indicators are used in offshore industry to identify critical
events that could lead to high consequence events. Examples of leading
indicators include number of standard violations and number of un-
planned process changes in the offshore platform. Another approach to
counter the difficulty pertaining to lack of data can be the use of newer
technologies such as experimental simulations and big-data processing
to generate artificial data (Pan et al., 2017).

In a general sense, the uncertainty associated with the results
deduced from HRA and HRA-based QRA decreases from first-generation
methods to third-generation methods. However, the uncertainty asso-
ciated with HRA and HRA-based QRA can be reduced by appropriate
selection of HRA methods and QRA methods that integrate these HRA
methods. As a part of this study, it was observed that HRA of offshore
emergency/transient scenarios can be effectively carried through first-
generation methods: SLIM, HEART, THERP and these HRA methods
can be integrated with QRA through FRAM. Similarly, HRA of normal
operation scenarios can be achieved through second-generation
methods such as SPAR-H and these methods can be integrated with
QRA through SBA, BN, and HCL.
3. Conclusion

In this study, various HRA methods applied in the offshore domain
and QRA methods that facilitate their integration were reviewed from
relevant safety journals and conference proceedings. It was observed
that first-generation HRA methods were largely applied in the offshore
domain for emergency scenarios whereas, second-generation HRA
methods were applied for normal design and operation. It was also
observed that the HRA based QRA of offshore domains were bound to
uncertainties and that these uncertainties can be reduced by imple-
menting appropriate HRA methods or appropriate QRA methods that are
integrated with these HRA methods (Fig. 4). Lastly, developing task-
specific HRA methods based on cognitive models that derive data
from databases relevant to the offshore domain and incorporate various
tasks and environmental PSFs can be an ideal path forward in reducing
uncertainties associated with HRA and HRA-based QRA.
